title: Structure for Multiple Locations
content_md:
# Structure for Multiple Locations

- Code: 8c266b3b
- Source: https://www.skool.com/ai-seo-mastery-pro/classroom/63a81f62?md=8c266b3b783246908270d88672365071
- Video: https://www.loom.com/share/ea44debfa73c43ec93761116c0d07977?hide_owner=true&hide_share=true&hide_title=true&hideEmbedTopBar=true
- Duration: 4m 55s

## Description

_(no description)_

transcript.text: So let me walk through how we would structure a multi-location site. So the
first caveat on this is whenever we look at a new client, a new perspective
client, and they have a multi-location setup, that means they have more than one
GBP, they've almost always linked all of their GBPs to the home page. So the
structure that we're going through here is going to be the most recommended way
to do this, and it gets ranking results faster than linking everything to the
home page. The reason I mention this is before you change the GBP landing page,
you must run a local rank map. If they're ranking, don't move the GBP landing page.
You will lose ranking, okay? I've looked at clients before that had eight
locations. All eight GBPs were pointed to the home page, six of them were ranking
really well, two of them weren't. The two that weren't ranking well, we created
new GBP landing pages. For those two, we left the other six pointed to the home
page. Do not make GBP landing page URL changes and expect not to lose rank
position if you have it. So the basic theory with this multi-location site
structure, and I know this looks really confusing, but this is essentially the
same thing that we're doing for a single location except we're treating the GBP
landing pages for every additional location as its own home page. So remember
landing page one, the primary keyword is going to be primary category city name.
The target keyword here is secondary category city name and this is
going to be service city name etc., right? So GBP landing page two, the target
keyword will be primary category city name, the category one, this is the first
secondary category, the target keyword will be secondary category city name and
this will be service city name. So you've basically built out the exact same
hierarchical structure under each one of the GBP landing pages. The supporting
content is supporting this service. If we needed supporting content for this
service, it would look exactly the same. We'd have supporting content linking to
that. Every GBP needs its own phone number. Now the way we place these here is
we place them underneath the locations. You don't need to do that if you want
to link to these other landing pages directly from the home page so that
these are basically promoted up one. That also is perfectly fine if you don't
want to put them underneath the locations page. These GBP landing pages are
basically copies of the home page. They function like a home page. Something
like 90% of traffic to a local business website is going to go to the GBP
landing page. We know that from looking at Google Analytics. So these landing
pages are essentially mini home pages that are structured with the exact
same core 30 structure as your primary GBP. Now the one that gets the home page
is going to rank the fastest and easiest. The home page is almost always the
most powerful URL on your domain. So if you had a location in Chicago and then
one in Algonquin and one in Naperville, the Chicago one is the one that we
would point to the home page. Algonquin in Naperville, we would point to two
and three. Caveat, of course, is if they already have the Algonquin location
first pointed to the home page and they're adding a Chicago location. If you
create a new landing page and move the Algonquin one to the new landing page,
you will lose rank position. Moving GBP landing pages to a new page will lose
rank position. Moving it away from the home page will lose rank position. So
that's a discussion to have with your client. It will be easier to rank in
Chicago if they're using their home page as the Chicago landing page. But if they
move their existing GBP away from the home page, it will lose rank. Okay? Hopefully
I'm making sense with this. If it is a particularly high competition area, then
what we often will do is create a second set of category and service pages where
the target keyword is just the category and service without the target city. Then
those would be the ones that live in the nav bar. So if we did that, we would have
GBP category one, two, three, all these different services. We'd have another set
of them that looked just like this. And that set is the set we would put in the
nav bar. If GBP location one was New York City and location two was Miami, we
would not want someone who lands in Miami to click on one of the pages in the
nav bar, which are targeting New York City. We don't do that for every client.
Just ones that are very, very high competition just because that's a, you
know, a significant increase to the amount of content that we would need to do
that. So all of these are editorial links. They can be nav bar. We try to minimize
nav bar links, but these are going to be editorial links on the copy just the
exact way we do the core 34 single location.
